beats.label: Title
beats.speaker_notes: Chapter 7. Three reasonable definitions of fairness. One dataset. You must pick one — and the theorem says you cannot have all three.
beats.on_slide_text: AI Engineering · Chapter 07 Fairness metrics: choosing a definition and defending it Three reasonable definitions. One dataset. Pick one — and learn why you cannot keep all three.

beats.label: The problem
beats.speaker_notes: You build a binary classifier on a population with two groups. You want it to be fair, so you write down requirements. Each sounds like fairness.
beats.on_slide_text: The problem You built a classifier. You sit down to write “make it fair.” A yes/no model — loan approval, recidivism, a hiring screen — applied to a population with two groups, A and B . The model predicts; the world produces outcomes; you compare the two. You write down three requirements. Each one, on its own, sounds exactly like what fairness should mean. I have to tell you: you cannot satisfy all three at once when the groups have different base rates — and on real data, they almost always do.

beats.label: The impossibility
beats.speaker_notes: This is a theorem, proved independently in 2016 by Kleinberg, Mullainathan and Raghavan, and by Chouldechova. It is the structural fact of the chapter.
beats.on_slide_text: The impossibility theorem · 2016 Demographic parity, equalized odds, and calibration parity cannot all hold on the same dataset when base rates differ. Not a tooling limit. Not a data problem. A structural fact — proved independently by Kleinberg, Mullainathan & Raghavan, and by Chouldechova. It forces a choice — and choices in engineering have to be defended.

beats.label: Three definitions
beats.on_slide_text: Part one Three definitions Each captures something fairness intuitively requires. Each is a different statement about the model.

beats.label: Demographic parity — equation
beats.speaker_notes: Demographic parity, a.k.a. statistical parity. A statement about the model's OUTPUTS, before comparing to ground truth.
beats.on_slide_text: Metric 01 · Equation Demographic parity also called statistical parity — a statement about the model’s outputs P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=a) = P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=b) In plain terms The model says “yes” to the same fraction of group A and group B. Equal treatment in the prediction itself — measured before anyone checks who was actually right. Values claim Outcomes should be proportional regardless of base rates. Structural redress over conditional accuracy.

beats.label: Demographic parity — example
beats.speaker_notes: Real-world example: loan approvals. The prediction itself carries the consequence.
beats.on_slide_text: Real-world example A bank approves loans at 40% in one neighborhood and 18% in another. Demographic parity says: the loan — the thing with real consequences, a house, a business — should be handed out at the same rate to both groups, full stop. It does not care whether default rates differ underneath. When you reach for parity, you are betting that the prediction’s payoff matters more than its conditional accuracy. That is a defensible bet — in some deployments. Not all.

beats.label: Equalized odds — equation
beats.speaker_notes: Equalized odds: equal TPR and equal FPR across groups. A statement about the model's ERRORS, conditional on the truth.
beats.on_slide_text: Metric 02 · Equation Equalized odds a statement about the model’s errors , conditional on the truth True-positive rate equal P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=1, A=a) = P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=1, A=b) False-positive rate equal P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=0, A=a) = P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=0, A=b) In plain terms The model makes its mistakes evenly . Among people who truly are positive, it catches the same share in both groups; among the truly negative, it falsely flags the same share. Values claim The cost of being wronged should be borne equally. Group membership should not change your risk of error.

beats.label: The triangle
beats.speaker_notes: The theorem as a triangle: you can satisfy any two. The third breaks. Watch the cycle rotate through which two you keep and which one breaks.
beats.on_slide_text: The theorem, drawn Satisfy any two. The third breaks.

beats.label: Arithmetic made visible
beats.speaker_notes: Same model, two threshold choices. Toggle between the calibration-satisfying and equalized-odds-satisfying versions. Calibration and equalized odds cannot both hold while base rates differ.
beats.on_slide_text: The trade-off, in bars Same model. Different threshold. You choose what breaks.

beats.label: Counterfactual fairness — procedure
beats.speaker_notes: Counterfactual fairness asks what would have happened to this individual under a different sensitive-attribute value, holding their background fixed. Computing it takes three steps: abduction, action, prediction.
beats.on_slide_text: Figure 7.3 What would have happened under a different group, holding background fixed? The three-step abduction → action → prediction procedure for counterfactual fairness.

beats.label: Close
beats.speaker_notes: Understand the math, choose the values, defend the integration. Next chapter: adversarial robustness.
beats.on_slide_text: The supervisory move Understand the math. Choose the values. Defend the integration. Three formally distinct metrics can be incompatible on one dataset when base rates differ. The impossibility is structural. The defense — connecting the chosen metric to the deployment, the cost-bearers, and the construct — is the work. Next — Chapter 8: adversarial robustness. Can a perturbation invisible to a human flip the model entirely?
